title: The grandmother who taught me to bargain
summary: Narrate a memory of an older woman who taught you how to bargain in a market, and reflect on what that exchange actually taught beyond price.
topic: Learning to bargain from an older person who knew the market
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) narrating how an older woman (grandmother, aunt, or family friend) taught you to bargain in a market, and reflect on what you actually learned. Use the past perfect for sequencing, present perfect for reflection, a relative clause, and 'however' or 'although'.
hintStarters: The first time my landlady took me through the souk in Marrakech, she handed me the money and said, very quietly, that she would not speak again until…
If I had tried to bargain on my own that morning, I would have walked away with the same price the previous tourist had been given, because…
Although it looked like a lesson in saving fifteen dirhams, what she was actually teaching me was something I have only properly understood years later, which is that…

hintVocab.meaningVi: gặp nhau ở giữa, thỏa hiệp
giá dành cho khách du lịch

sampleText:
The first time my landlady, Lalla Hafsa, took me through the souk in Marrakech, she handed me a few coins, pointed at a stack of brass lanterns at the end of an alley, and said very quietly that she would not speak again until I had finished. I was twenty-three and had been in the country for less than a month. I had assumed bargaining was a kind of mathematical competition: you said a low number, he said a high one, and you met in the middle. Lalla Hafsa had grown up in this market and had been buying brass for fifty years, and she clearly thought I had misunderstood almost everything about what was supposed to happen. She let me make every mistake in the first ten minutes. I named a price too quickly. I looked too eager. I forgot to greet the vendor in Arabic before talking about money. She watched, said nothing, and let the conversation collapse.

When the lantern seller laughed and said, in French, 'come back when you understand the game,' she finally stepped forward. She did not raise her voice or argue. She greeted him by name. She asked about his daughter, who had been ill the previous month. She admired three lanterns she had no intention of buying. Then, almost as an afterthought, she mentioned the one I had been pointing at and asked what he could do for an old neighbour. The price he named was less than half what he had quoted me twenty minutes earlier, and he said it without any sense of having been pressured. Although critics of bargaining cultures often describe them as inefficient or stressful, what I watched that morning was the opposite of inefficient: it was a system in which the price of an object depended on the relationship around it, and the relationship had been built over forty years of small exchanges.

What I have come to understand, in the decade since, is that Lalla Hafsa was not teaching me how to argue. She was teaching me how to read a situation slowly enough to see who was actually in front of me. The vendor had a daughter, a history with this family, a sense of his own dignity, and a working day that was about more than the next sale. Bargaining badly meant treating him as a vending machine; bargaining well meant treating him as a person whose price reflected things that had nothing to do with the lantern. However, this is not a skill I have fully absorbed even now. In a hurry I still default to the wrong rhythm. The difference is that I now know what I am failing at, and that knowing changes how I move through markets, shops, and even conversations that have nothing to do with money.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect to show what was already true before a key moment
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I had assumed bargaining was a kind of mathematical competition' và 'she had been buying brass for fifty years' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành để mô tả các sự kiện và trạng thái đã có trước khoảnh khắc chính trong câu chuyện. Đây là kỹ thuật quan trọng để dựng nền cho một câu chuyện ở B1.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I had assumed bargaining was a kind of mathematical competition' and 'she had been buying brass for fifty years' use the past perfect (simple and continuous) to describe events and states already in place before the key moment of the story. This is a critical technique for setting up a B1 narrative.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'until' to mark a turning point in past time
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'she would not speak again until I had finished' dùng 'until' với quá khứ hoàn thành ('had finished') để chỉ thời điểm kết thúc của một trạng thái — bà ấy im lặng cho đến điểm đó. Cấu trúc này rất hữu ích để dựng tình huống có nhịp độ rõ ràng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'she would not speak again until I had finished' uses 'until' with the past perfect ('had finished') to mark the endpoint of a state — she would stay silent up to that moment. This structure is very useful for setting up a clearly paced situation.

sampleTranslationVi:
Lần đầu tiên bà chủ nhà của tôi, Lalla Hafsa, dẫn tôi qua khu chợ ở Marrakech, bà đưa cho tôi vài đồng xu, chỉ vào một chồng đèn đồng ở cuối một con hẻm, và nói rất khẽ rằng bà sẽ không nói gì nữa cho đến khi tôi xong. Tôi hai mươi ba tuổi và đã ở đất nước này chưa đầy một tháng. Tôi đã cho rằng trả giá là một loại cuộc thi toán học: bạn nêu một con số thấp, ông ta nêu một con số cao, và bạn gặp nhau ở giữa. Lalla Hafsa đã lớn lên trong khu chợ này và đã mua đồ đồng năm mươi năm, và rõ ràng bà nghĩ tôi đã hiểu sai gần như mọi thứ về điều được cho là sẽ xảy ra. Bà để tôi mắc mọi sai lầm trong mười phút đầu. Tôi nêu một mức giá quá nhanh. Tôi trông quá háo hức. Tôi quên chào người bán bằng tiếng Ả Rập trước khi nói về tiền. Bà nhìn, không nói gì, và để cuộc trò chuyện sụp đổ.

Khi người bán đèn cười và nói, bằng tiếng Pháp, 'quay lại khi anh hiểu trò chơi,' bà cuối cùng bước tới. Bà không lớn giọng hay tranh cãi. Bà chào ông ấy bằng tên. Bà hỏi thăm con gái ông, người đã ốm tháng trước. Bà ngắm ba chiếc đèn mà bà không có ý định mua. Sau đó, gần như như một suy nghĩ thoáng qua sau cùng, bà nhắc đến chiếc đèn tôi đã chỉ vào và hỏi ông có thể làm gì cho một người hàng xóm cũ. Cái giá ông ấy nêu ít hơn một nửa cái giá ông ấy đã báo cho tôi hai mươi phút trước, và ông ấy nói nó không có cảm giác đã bị ép. Mặc dù những người chỉ trích văn hóa trả giá thường miêu tả nó là kém hiệu quả hay căng thẳng, điều tôi quan sát sáng hôm đó là ngược lại với kém hiệu quả: đó là một hệ thống trong đó giá của một món đồ phụ thuộc vào mối quan hệ xung quanh nó, và mối quan hệ đã được xây dựng qua bốn mươi năm trao đổi nhỏ.

Điều tôi đã dần hiểu, trong thập kỷ kể từ đó, là Lalla Hafsa không dạy tôi cách tranh luận. Bà đang dạy tôi cách đọc một tình huống đủ chậm để thấy ai thực sự đang đứng trước mặt mình. Người bán có một cô con gái, một lịch sử với gia đình này, một cảm giác về phẩm giá của mình, và một ngày làm việc không chỉ là về lần bán tiếp theo. Trả giá kém nghĩa là đối xử với ông ấy như một máy bán hàng tự động; trả giá tốt nghĩa là đối xử với ông ấy như một con người có cái giá phản ánh những thứ không liên quan gì đến chiếc đèn. Tuy nhiên, đây không phải là kỹ năng tôi đã tiếp thu trọn vẹn ngay cả bây giờ. Khi vội, tôi vẫn theo nhịp điệu sai. Sự khác biệt là tôi giờ biết mình đang thất bại ở chỗ nào, và việc biết đó thay đổi cách tôi di chuyển qua các chợ, cửa hàng, và thậm chí các cuộc trò chuyện không liên quan gì đến tiền.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the past perfect correctly?
options: I had assumed bargaining was a kind of mathematical competition.
I have assumed bargaining was a kind of mathematical competition.
I had assume bargaining was a kind of mathematical competition.
I had been assume bargaining was a kind of mathematical competition.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect = 'had + past participle'; past participle of 'assume' is 'assumed'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the past perfect continuous correctly?
options: She had been buying brass for fifty years.
She has been buying brass for fifty years.
She had buying brass for fifty years.
She had been bought brass for fifty years.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect continuous = 'had been + -ing'. 'Has been buying' is present perfect continuous, which does not fit a past narrative.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'until' with the past perfect correctly?
options: She would not speak again until I had finished.
She would not speak again until I have finished.
She would not to speak again until I had finished.
She would not speak again until I had finishing.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Until' marks the endpoint; 'had finished' is past perfect (past + past participle). 'Would' is followed by the bare infinitive.
mcQuestions.hint: Past perfect after 'until' in a past narrative; modal + bare infinitive.

options: Although critics of bargaining cultures often describe them as inefficient, what I watched was the opposite.
Although critics of bargaining cultures often describe them as inefficient, but what I watched was the opposite.
Although, critics of bargaining cultures often describe them as inefficient what I watched was the opposite.
Critics of bargaining cultures often describe them as inefficient, although however what I watched was the opposite.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling with 'however'.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'although' alone; do not pair with 'but' or 'however'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'less than half what he had quoted' correctly?
options: The price was less than half what he had quoted me twenty minutes earlier.
The price was less than half that he had quoted me twenty minutes earlier.
The price was less than half what he has quoted me twenty minutes earlier.
The price was less than half what he had quoting me twenty minutes earlier.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What' acts as 'the thing which' — a free relative pronoun. Past perfect ('had quoted') correctly places the quoting before the current moment.
mcQuestions.hint: Free relative 'what' = 'the thing(s) which'; past perfect for earlier action.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'let + object + base verb' correctly?
options: She let me make every mistake in the first ten minutes.
She let me to make every mistake in the first ten minutes.
She let me making every mistake in the first ten minutes.
She let me made every mistake in the first ten minutes.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Let + object + bare infinitive' is the fixed structure. No 'to', no '-ing', no past tense after 'let'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'come to understand' correctly?
options: What I have come to understand, in the decade since, is that she was teaching me how to read a situation.
What I have came to understand, in the decade since, is that she was teaching me how to read a situation.
What I have come understanding, in the decade since, is that she was teaching me how to read a situation.
What I came to understand, in the decade since now, is that she was teaching me how to read a situation.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'have come to + base verb' shows gradual realisation continuing to now. 'Have came' is wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: Past participle of 'come' is 'come'; followed by 'to + base verb'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'treat someone as' correctly?
options: Bargaining badly meant treating him as a vending machine.
Bargaining badly meant treating him like as a vending machine.
Bargaining badly meant treating him to a vending machine.
Bargaining badly meant treating him for a vending machine.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Treat someone as + noun' is the correct collocation meaning 'consider/handle them as if they were'. 'Like' would be informal; 'to' and 'for' are wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Treat someone as + noun' is the standard structure.

options: The price of an object depended on the relationship around it.
The price of an object depended of the relationship around it.
The price of an object depended to the relationship around it.
The price of an object depended for the relationship around it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Depend on + noun' is the correct collocation; 'of', 'to', and 'for' are all wrong.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'default to + noun' correctly?
options: In a hurry I still default to the wrong rhythm.
In a hurry I still default at the wrong rhythm.
In a hurry I still default for the wrong rhythm.
In a hurry I still default into the wrong rhythm.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Default to + noun' is the correct collocation meaning 'fall back on automatically'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Default to + noun' is the fixed collocation.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'have fully absorbed' correctly?
options: This is not a skill I have fully absorbed even now.
This is not a skill I fully have absorbed even now.
This is not a skill I have fully absorbing even now.
This is not a skill I had fully absorbed even now.
mcQuestions.explanation: Mid-position 'fully' goes between 'have' and the past participle 'absorbed'. Past perfect 'had' would mismatch the present-time reference 'even now'.
mcQuestions.hint: Mid-position adverb: auxiliary + adverb + past participle; present perfect matches 'even now'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If bargaining is a form of relationship-building rather than a way to lower a price, why do societies that adopt fixed-price culture often feel they have gained something — and what, exactly, have they given up?
